questionText:
1. Consider the following statements with reference to Satavahana rulers:
1. King Hala was the founder of the Satavahana dynasty.
2. They issued a large number of gold coins.
3. Prakrit was the official language of the Satavahana kingdom.
Which of the statements given above are incorrect?
explanation:
Simuka (60 BC- 37 BC) was the founder of the Satavahana dynasty and is mentioned as the first king in a list of royals in a Satavahana at Naneghat inscription. Also referred to as Balipuccha in some texts.
King Hala: Sources: Mentioned in Matsya Purana as the 17th ruler of the Satavahana dynasty. Hala compiled “The Gatha Sattasai”, a collection of poems. Gunadhya (composed Brihatkatha) was a scholar in his court.
The most important of the native successors of the Mauryas in the Deccan and in central India were the Satavahanas. The early Satavahana kings appeared in Maharashtra, where most of their early inscriptions have been found. They set up their power in the upper Godavari valley and gradually extended their power over Karnataka and Andhra. So, statement 1 is not correct.
The Satavahanas may have used gold as bullion, for they did not issue gold coins as the Kushans did. They issued mostly coins of lead, which were found in the Deccan. They also issued potin (a base metal alloy used in coins. It is typically a mixture of copper, tin and lead), copper and bronze money. So, statement 2 is not correct.
The official language of the Satavahanas was Prakrit. All inscriptions were composed in this language and written in the Brahmi script, as was the case in Ashokan time. So, statement 3 is correct.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
Knowledge Box:
Some of the inscriptions of Satavahanas include:
Amaravati inscription: Written by the second Pulumavi, this is the first Satavahana inscription in Andhra Pradesh. This indicates that the Satavahana empire spread to Andhra during the Pulumavi II period
Chebrolu inscription: Issued in 207 A.D. by Satavahana king Vijaya, this is the earliest datable Sanskrit inscription from South India.
Nasik inscription (Cave No.19 of the Pandavleni Caves) highlights the achievements of Gautamiputra Satakarni. Issued during the reign of Kanha (100-70 BCE), this is one of the earliest known Satavahana inscriptions. It is also known as Nashik prashasti in which his mother Gautami Balasri’s has given a eulogy, which credits him with extensive military conquests. It gives a detailed account of the Satavahana dynasty.
subject: History

questionText:
2. Consider the following pairs:
S no. Kingdom Territorial extent
1. Sakas Eastern India
2. Satavahanas Northern Gangetic Plains
3. Pandyas Tamil Nadu
4. Cheras Parts of Karnataka
How many of the above pairs is/are correctly matched?
explanation:
Pair 1 is incorrectly matched: The Sakas had established their powers in the upper Deccan and western India. Indo-Scythians (also called Indo-Sakas) were a group of nomadic Iranian peoples of Scythian origin who migrated from Central Asia southward into the northwestern Indian subcontinent, precisely into the modern-day South Asian regions of Afghanistan, Pakistan and northern India the Sakas also captured Gandhara and Taxila, and migrated to North India. The most famous Indo-Scythian king was Maues. An Indo-Scythian kingdom was established in Mathura (200 BC – 400 AD)
Pair 2 is incorrectly matched: The Satavahanas, also referred to as the Andhras. Most modern scholars believe that the Satavahana rule began in the late second century BCE and lasted until the early third century CE.The dynasty had different capital cities at different times, including Pratishthana (Paithan) and Amaravati (Dharanikota). Simuka was an ancient Indian king belonging to the Satavahana dynasty, which ruled the Deccan region. He is mentioned as the first king in a list of royals in a Satavahana inscription at Nanaghat. The early Satvahana kings appeared in Maharashtra and later extended their power over Karnataka and Andhra.
Pair 3 is correctly matched: The Pandyan dynasty, also referred to as the Pandyas of Madurai, was an ancient Tamil dynasty of South India, and among the four great kingdoms of Tamilakam, the other three being the Pallavas, the Cholas and the Cheras.The Pandyas ruled over modern Tamil Nadu.
Pair 4 is incorrectly matched:The Cheras of the early historical period (c. second century BCE – c. third century CE) are known to have had their original centre at Kuttanad in Kerala, and harbours at Muchiri (Muziris) and Thondi (Tyndis) on the Indian Ocean coast (Kerala) and Kongunadu. They governed the area of Malabar Coast between Alappuzha in the south to Kasaragod in the north The Cheras had reigned over the modern Kerala.
Therefore, option a is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
3. Consider the following statements with reference to Sangam literature:
1. Tolkappiyam is a treatise on Tamil grammar.
2. Tirukkural was written by Sittalai Sattanar.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
The Tamil Sangams were assemblies of Tamil scholars and poets. Apart from the heroic anthologies, the classified corpus of Sangam literature includes Tolkappiyam, a treatise on Tamil grammar and Patinenkizkanakku, the eighteen didactic texts also. There is no firm evidence to assign the authorship of this treatise to any one author. Tholkapiyam, some traditionally believe, was written by a single author named Tolkappiyar, a disciple of Vedic sage Agastya mentioned in the Rigveda. The Tolkappiyam belongs to second Sangam period. So, statement 1 is correct.
Thiruvalluvar, one of the greatest authors in Indian history, wrote Thirukkural more than 2000 years ago. The Thirukkural is one of the most revered ancient works in the Tamil language. It is considered a 'common creed', providing a guide for human morals and betterment in life. Satthanar or Chithalai Satthanar was the Tamil poet who composed the epic Manimekalai. Hence, Statement 2 is incorrect.
The Tamil literary tradition is independent of the classical Sanskritic literary tradition of India. It represents a parallel linguistic tradition in relation to Sanskrit. However, the development of the Tamil language and literature was never a process in isolation. Even the earliest stratum of Tamil literature contains influences of Sanskrit.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
4. Consider the following statements with reference to Sangam age:
1. Agriculture was the chief occupation with very little advancement in the handicrafts industry.
2. The Vellalar was the social group in the societal hierarchy which was prohibited from practicing agriculture.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Three important chiefdoms developed in the ecological zone referred to as the marutam in the Sangam poems. This area comprised the fertile river valleys on the plains. The peasant groups which settled here brought about the transition from hunting and pastoralism to agriculture based economy.
An important source for studying the polity of Sangam period is Tirukkural (a post sangam work) by Tiruvalluvar. Tiruvalluvar refers to the Nadu (rastra) as the foundation of the polity. He points out that the king's treasury should be stocked through various sources of income viz. land revenue, transit duties, custom and also through annexation.
Agriculture was the chief occupation where rice was the most common crop. The handicraft included weaving, metal works and carpentry, ship building and making of ornaments using beads, stones and ivory. These were in great demand in the internal and external trade that was at its peak during the Sangam period. A high expertise was attained in spinning and weaving of cotton and silk clothes. These were in great demand in the western world especially for the cotton clothes woven at Uraiyur. So, statement 1 is incorrect.
The Vellalar (agriculturists) were categorised into those who themselves cultivated the land and those who employed others to cultivate the land. So, statement 2 is not correct.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
5. With reference to Tamil epics, Silappadikaram and Manimekalai, consider the following statements:
1. Both Silappadikaram and Manimekalai were written by Ilango Idgal.
2. Both are Jain treatises.
3. Both literary works are a result of the first Sangam Assembly around 2nd-3rd century BCE.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
Silappadhikaram, a famous Tamil epic, was composed by a poet named Ilango Idgal, around 1800 years ago. It is the story of a merchant named Kovalan, who lived in Puhar and fell in love with a courtesan named Madhavi, neglecting his wife, Kannagi. Manimekalai was composed by Sattanar around 1400 years ago. This describes the story of the daughter of Kovalan and Madhavi. Akam stands for the interior, the individual, the feminine, and the varied phases of love. Women dominate the Akam. Puram stands for the exterior, the world, the masculine, violence and heroism. It is male-dominated. So, statement 1 is incorrect.
Silappadhikaram uses the unique non-Sanskritic poetic sequence that incorporates Iyal (poetry), Isai (music) and Natakam (dance) and is interspersed with prose sections. The use of the Akam and Puram forms is strategic in Silappadhikaram.
The presence of women as central characters in the Silappadhikaram and the Manimekalai unlike in Sanskrit epics where the male protagonist is central to the plot is the influence of Buddhism and Jainism on the epical tradition of Tamil Nadu.
Both epics have been composed after the Sangam period during the Buddhist and the Jain periods of Tamil Nadu. The Silappatikaran is a Jain text with larger overtones of Jain philosophy and the Manimekalai is a Buddhist text with a heavy influence of Buddhist theology. So, statements 2 & 3 are not correct.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
6. With reference to the social conditions in the Sangam Age, consider the following statements:
1. Karpu or chaste life was considered the highest virtue of women.
2. The practice of sati was completely prohibited in the society.
3. The women had no freedom to choose their life partners.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
Women in the Sangam period was known to have been educated. Women poets like Avvaiyar, Nachchellaiyar, and Kakkai Padiniyar flourished in this period and contributed to Tamil literature.
The courage of women was appreciated in many poems. Karpu or chaste life (celibate) was considered the highest virtue of women. So, statement 1 is correct.
Love marriage was a common practice. Women were allowed to choose their life partners. However, the life of widows was miserable. Widow remarriage was not allowed.Women who lost their husbands are called Widows and their life was so miserable and they were asked to kill themselves in the funeral pyre of their husbands So, statement 3 is not correct.
Women are also described as engaged in various economic activities such as paddy plantation, cattle rearing, basket-making, spinning, etc.
However, the cruel practice of Sati was also prevalent in Tamil society, and it was known as Tippayadal. Although, it was not obligatory as there are references of widows present in society. Their position was miserable as they were prohibited to decorate themselves or participate in any form of amusement. So, statement 2 is not correct.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: 7. In the context of sangam age, the term ‘Viraliyar’ refers to:
options: b) singing poets in royal courts
explanation:
Panar and Viraliyar were the singing bards (poets) in royal courts of the contemporary kingdoms during the Sangam age. They were experts in folk songs and folk dances. Poetry, music, and dancing was popular among the people of the Sangam age. Liberal donations were given to poets by the kings, chieftains, and nobles.
A variety of Yazhs and drums are referred to in the Sangam literature. Dancing was performed by Kanigaiyar. Koothu was the most popular entertainment for people during this age.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
8. With reference of Sangam history, consider the following statements:
1. The Vedar were the headman of small villages providing obligatory services to bigger chiefs like Cheras, Cholas and Pandyas in their campaigns.
2. The institution of gift-giving was integral to the practice of booty redistribution during the Sangam age.
Which of the statements given above is/are incorrect?
explanation:
Statement 1 is incorrect: In Tamilaham, three types of chiefdoms existed: kizar (small chiefs), velir (larger chiefs), and vedar (the largest chiefs). The Kizar, leading small villages bound by kinship, are frequently mentioned in poems, identified by their village names. Examples include Arnkantur-kizar or UrnturKizar. Some Kizar were subjugated by larger chiefs and obliged to serve in campaigns. Poems depict Kizar providing obligatory services (vidutozil) to bigger chiefs like Cheras, Cholas, and Pandyas, who, in return, rewarded them with gifts, including predatory control over subjugated villages.
Statement 2 is correct: Chiefs, irrespective of their size or status, had to engage in plunder raids to meet the needs of their communities. The acquired plunder was then distributed among warriors, bards, mendicants, and their kinsmen. The institution of gift-giving (Kodai) was integral to the practice of booty redistribution.
Therefore, option a is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
9. With reference to the megalithic culture in India, consider the following statements:
1. Megaliths were constructed either as burial sites or commemorative memorials.
2. Megaliths are only restricted to the sites of peninsular India.
3. The megalithic culture in present day India is extinct and not followed by any community.
How many of the above statements are incorrect?
explanation:
Megaliths were constructed either as burial sites or commemorative (non-sepulchral) memorials. The former are sites with actual burial remains, such as dolmenoid cists (box-shaped stone burial chambers), cairn circles (stone circles with defined peripheries) and capstones (distinctive mushroom-shaped burial chambers found mainly in Kerala). The urn or the sarcophagus containing the mortal remains was usually made of terracotta. Non-sepulchral megaliths include memorial sites such as menhirs. (The line separating the two is a bit blurry, since remains have been discovered underneath otherwise non-sepulchral sites, and vice versa). So, statement 1 is correct.
Around 2,200 megalithic sites can be found in peninsular India itself, most of them unexcavated. Megaliths are spread across the Indian subcontinent, though the bulk of them are found in peninsular India, concentrated in the states of Maharashtra (mainly in Vidarbha), Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Kerala, Andhra Pradesh and Telangana. Some of the megalithic sites in Meghalaya are Nartiang, Cherrapunji, Jowai, Maoflong, Lailugkot etc. So, statement 2 is incorrect.
Even today, a living megalithic culture endures among some tribes such as the Gonds of central India and the Khasis of Meghalaya. The tribes who still include megaliths in their religious beliefs are the Gadabas, Gonds, Kurumbas, Marias, Mundas, Savaras, Garos, Khasis, Nagas, Karbis, Tiwas, and Marams. Some of the megalithic sites in the Meghalaya are Nartiang, Cherrapunji, Jowai, Maoflong, Lailugkot etc. These groups still construct megalithic monuments for the dead. ‘Megalithism’ may be considered as a living tradition. So, statement 3 is not correct.
So, two of the above statements are incorrect.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
Knowledge Box
Menhirs
These are upright stones from the Megalithic period. They are erected above a burial site or near a burial site as a memorial.
It could also exist as part of a group of similar stones. They have different sizes with uneven and square shapes, often tapering towards the top.
Menhirs are widely distributed across different continents viz., Europe, Africa, and Asia, but are most commonly found in Western Europe; in particular in Ireland, Great Britain and Brittany.
The Gonds, Kurumbas, Morias and Savaras plant and worship stone menhirs and sometimes erect wooden pillars. Some of these wooden pillars are curved with a rounded projection at the top to represent the human head. These tribes consider the stone menhirs and the wooden posts to represent their gods, or occasionally, the spirit of the dead.
subject: History

questionText:
10. Consider the following:
1. Cheras
2. Kakatiyas
3. Pallavas
4. Cholas
5. Pandyas
Which among the following were called ‘Muvendar’ in the Sangam literature?
explanation:
In Sangam poems, Cheras, Cholas and Pandyas were known as Muvendar, 'the three crowned kings'. They controlled major agrarian territories, trade routes and towns. So, points 1, 4 and 5 are correct.
The Cholas controlled the central and northern parts of Tamil Nadu. Their core area of rule was the Kaveri delta, later known as Cholamandalam. Thanjavur (Tanjore) was the capital of Cholas.
The Cheras controlled the central and northern parts of Kerala and the Kongu region of Tamil Nadu. Vanji was their capital and the ports of the west coast, Musiri and Tondi, were under their control.
The Pandyas ruled from Madurai. Korkai was their main port, located near the confluence of Thampraparani with the Bay of Bengal.
The Kakatiyas were the most powerful Kings of Telangana during the 12th and 13th Centuries. After the decline of the Sangam Age in the Tamil country, the Kalabhra rule lasted for about 250 years. Thereafter, the Pallavas established their kingdom in Tondaimandalam with its capital at Kanchipuram. Thus, both the Kakatiya and the Pallavas were not the Muvendar. So, points 2 and 3 are not correct.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
11. Consider the following pairs:
Sl. No. Port city Kingdom
1. Korkai : Pandyas
2. Muziris : Cheras
3. Puhar : Cholas
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
explanation:
Korkai: It was a seaport of Pandyas. It is located in present day Tamil Nadu. Maduraikkanji written by Mangudi Maruthanar describes the socio-economic condition of the Pandya country including the flourishing seaport of Korkai. So, pair 1 is correctly matched.
Muziris: It has been referred to in the ancient Tamil text, the Purananuru as a port city under the Chera rulers. The exact location of Muziris is still not known to historians and archaeologists, but it is generally thought to be at Pattanam, an archaeological site north of Cochin in Kerala, on the banks of River Periyar. So, pair 2 is correctly matched.
Poompuhar/Puhar: It was a flourishing ancient port city known as Kaveri Poompattinam. It served as the capital of the Early Chola kings in Tamilakam. Puhar is located near the mouth of the Kaveri River, on the sea coast in Tamil Nadu. It was submerged due to “kadalkol” or rising sea levels. So, pair 3 is correctly matched.
So, all three pairs are correctly matched.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
12. Consider the following statements with reference to the Sangam age:
1. The Sangam literature is a compilation of purely religious texts written in Sanskrit.
2. The Sangams were held at Madurai and Kapadapuram under the patronage of Pandyas.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
The Early Historic period of the Tamil country, which is also called the Sangam Age, is generally placed between fifth century B.C. and fifth century A.D.
The Sangam literature is the oral bardic literature of the ancient Tamils. The most interesting feature of Sangam Literature is the fact that while most of the early literary traditions were religious writings in some form or the other, Sangam Literature is not religious in that sense. The poems belonging to what is called Sangam Literature are based on two main themes – love / akam, and heroism/ praise of Kings and their deeds/ puram. So, statement 1 is not correct.
The three Sangams are considered to have been convened under the patronage of the Pandya kings of Madurai. The First Sangam, is believed to be held at Madurai, attended by gods and legendary sages. The Second Sangam was held at Kapadapuram and only Tolkappiyam survives from this. The Third Sangam was also held at Madurai. So, statement 2 is correct.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
13. Consider the following statements with reference to Chola King, Karaikala:
1. He defeated the combined army of Pandya and Chera rulers in the Battle of Venni Parantalai.
2. Great Anicut built on Krishna River was built during his regime.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Early Cholas ruled over the kingdom of Tamil Nadu during the pre and post-Sangam period (c. 600 BCE - 300 CE). Their early capitals were Urayur/Uraiyur (Tiruchirapalli) and Kaveripattinam.
Karikala Chola was the most prominent of the early Cholas who is referred to in a number of Sangam poetic compositions.
In the Battle of Venni Parantalai (c. 130 CE), the Karikala was said to have defeated a confederacy of kings of the Pandya and Chera empires and eleven minor chieftains. So, statement 1 is correct.
Kallanai (also known as the Grand Anicut) is an ancient dam built by Karikala of Chola dynasty in 150 CE. It is built (in running water) across the Kaveri River flowing from Tiruchirapalli District to Thanjavur district, Tamil Nadu, India. Hence, Statement 2 is incorrect.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: 14. With reference to the history of ancient India, ‘Aham and Puram’ were:
options: a) compositions of the Sangam literature
b) poets in the court of Vasishthiputra Pulumavi
c) types of land grants given by the Chola rulers
d) major trading towns of the Pandya kingdom
explanation:
Sangam literature consists of the earliest Tamil works, the ten poems, the eight anthologies (Ettutogai) and the eighteen minor and major works and the three epics.
Pattupattu or Ten Poems: Murugarruppadai (by Nakkirar), Sirupanarruppadai (by Nattattanar), Perumbanarruppadai, Maduraikkanji (by Mangudi Marudam), Pattinappalai (by Kannan), and other works, come in this category. The poetry in the Pattupattu was divided into two main groups: Aham (deals with matters strictly limited to one aspect of subjective experience viz., love) and Puram (deals with matters capable of externalisation or objectification).
Eighteen Minor Works or Padinenkilkanakku: These works are called ‘minor works’ because the poems in these are shorter in form than those in the Ettutogai and Pattuppattu. The most important among these are the Tirukkural by Tiruvalluvar (known as the Bible of Tamil Land’, it is a compound of the Dharmasastra, the Arthasastra and the Kamasutra), the Naladiyar, the Palamoli by Munnururai Araiyar, the Acharakkovai etc.
Melkanakku or Eighteen Major Works: It consists of eight anthologies and ten idylls (short poems). These texts are considered works of heroic poetry in which heroes are glorified and perpetual wars and cattle raids are frequently mentioned. They show that early Tamil people were primarily pastoral. Traces of early megalithic life also appear in the texts.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
15. Consider the following statements with reference to the Sangam literature:
1. The emotions portrayed in Sangam poetry are expressed through physical features like seashore and forest.
2. There were no women poets who had contributed to the Sangam literature.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Sangam Literature refers to a body of ancient Tamil writings probably produced during the chankams/ literary academies located in Madurai, Tamil Nadu from the 4th to the 1st Century”. Sangam Literature is also referred to as early classical Tamil literature with works dating between 400 BCE to 250 CE.
The most interesting feature of Sangam Literature is the fact that while most of the early literary traditions were religious writings in some form or the other, Sangam Literature is not religious in that sense. The poems belonging to what is called Sangam Literature are based on two main themes – love / akam, and heroism/ praise of Kings and their deeds/ puram.
The emotions portrayed in Sangam poetry are expressed through physical geography like the hills, the seashore, the wasteland, the forest and the low land or the marshes. They are called Thinai and they form in the prosody analysis in the third section of the Tolkappiyam. So, statement 1 is correct.
The topographical related emotional Tenai’s in these poems include:
Kurinchi (The Hills) signifying lovers’ meetings
Mullai (The Forest) signifying happiness through physical and emotional union
Marutham (The Low land) signifying a lover’s unfaithful attitude
Neythal (The Sea Shore) signifying secret meetings at the sea shore and lovers’ anxiety
Paalai (The Waste land) signifying a lovers frustrating journey through the deserted landscape.
Women were taught Iyal (literature), Isai (music) and Natakam (Drama) and men were given training for developing their physical stamina. Women who were proficient in Iyal, Isai and Natakam were known as ‘Mudukkuravai’.
Sangam literature says that there were 26 poetesses in that period. Some of the famous Sangam poetesses were Avvaiyar, Kakkai Pattiniyar, Okkur Masathiyar, Natchellaiyar, Nappasalaiyar, Nannagaiyar, Anjil Anjiyar, Adi Manthi, Paari Mahalir, Kopperum Pendu, Pon Mudiyar, Mudanthama Kanniyar, Vennikuyathiyar and Velliiveedhiyar. So, statement 2 is not correct.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
16. With reference to Satavahana period, consider the following statements:
1. The administration of Satavahanas was highly centralized in nature.
2. The practice of offering land grants to the Brahmins was absent during this period.
3. The Satavahanas inscriptions mostly refer to the names of their mothers rather than those of their fathers.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
Satavahanas established a powerful kingdom in the first century BCE in the Deccan region. They ruled over parts of Andhra, Maharashtra, Karnataka and Madhya Pradesh.
According to Purana tradition, it was Simuka Satavahana who established Satavahana power. Gautamiputra Satakarni was the greatest of the Satavahana kings. He defeated the Shaka ruler Nahapana and reissued the coins of Nahapana with his own royal insignia. The inscription of his mother Gautami Balashri at Nashik mentions him as the conqueror of the Shakas, Pahlavas, and Yavanas. He is also said to have performed the prestigious Vedic Ashvamedha sacrifice.
Satavahans retained some of the administrative features of Mauryas. The king was represented as the upholder of dharma. They had a decentralised administration.
Aharas: sub-divisions of the Kingdom into districts (administered by Amatyas or Mahamatras).
Rashtras: The administrative divisions were also called Rashtras, and their officials were called Maharashtrikas.
Grama: It was the lowest level of administration under the charge of a Gramika or Gaulmika (head of the military regiment consisting of nine chariots, nine elephants, twenty-five horses and forty-five-foot soldiers). So, statement 1 is not correct.
Offering land grants was an important development of the Satavahana times. The beneficiaries of these grants were mostly Buddhists and Brahmins. The Naneghat inscription refers to tax exemptions given to the lands granted to Buddhist monks. This led to the beginning of priestly groups attaining higher status. These land donations created a group of people who did not cultivate, but owned land. This led to the development of land-based social hierarchy and divisions in the society. So, statement 2 is not correct.
The social structure of the Deccan under the Satavahanas shows many features which are different from those prescribed in the Sanskrit texts such as the Manusmriti. For example, many inscriptions of the Satavahana rulers mention the names of their mothers rather than those of their fathers, such as Gautamiputra Satakarni. This is not in keeping with the Dharmasastras which state that in the approved forms of marriage the bride acquires the gotra of her husband and loses that of the father. The other interesting feature of the inscriptions is that the Satavahanas refer to themselves as unique Brahmanas who crushed the pride of the Kshatriyas. So, statement 3 is correct.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
17. Consider the following statements with reference to the Satavahanas:
1. Nagarjuna was a contemporary of the Satavahana King Gautamiputra Satakarni.
2. The Katakas were military camps that served as administrative centers.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
The most important of the native successors of the Mauryas in the Deccan and in central India were the Satavahanas. In the north, the Mauryas were succeeded by the Shungas followed by the Kanvas. The Satavahanas are considered to be the same as the Andhras mentioned in the Puranas. The Satavahanas retained some administrative structures of Ashokan times. Their administrative divisions were also called Rashtra, and their high officials were styled Maharashtrikas.
Nagarjuna was a contemporary of the Satavahana King Gautamiputra Satakarni (130-150 A.D.) and spent the latter part of his career in the monastery built by him at Bhramaragiri (Sriparvata). Nagarjuna is widely considered to be the founder of the Madhyamaka school of Buddhist philosophy and a defender of the Mahna movement. The Manjuasrimulakalpa and Tibetan sources contend that Nagarjuna had the Amaravati stupa renovated, enclosed and ornamented with the assistance of this King. So, statement 1 is correct.
The military character of Satavahana rule is evident from the common use of such terms as Kataka and Skandhavara in their inscriptions. These were military camps and settlements which served as administrative centres when the king was there. Thus, coercion played a key role in the Satavahana administration. So, statement 2 is correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
18. With reference to the King Hala of Satavahanas, consider the following statements:
1. He wrote Gathasaptasati in the Sanskrit language.
2. He is referred to as the ‘Lord of the Deccan’ in Queen Naganika’s inscription at Naneghat.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Satavahanas:
The Satavahanas established their independent rule after the decline of the Mauryas. Their rule lasted for about 450 years. The Puranas and inscriptions are important sources for the history of Satavahanas kings.
The seventeenth king of the Satavahana dynasty was Hala. He reigned for a period of five years. Hala became famous for his book Gathasaptasati, also called Sattasai. It contains 700 verses in Prakrit language. So, statement 1 is not correct.
Nasik and Naneghat inscriptions throw light on the reign of Satakarni-I. He was the first powerful ruler of the dynasty and has been referred to as the Lord of the Deccan (Dakshinapathapati) in Queen Naganika’s inscription at Naneghat. So, statement 2 is not correct.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
Knowledge Box
Gathasaptasati
The name Gathasaptashati means 700 verses written in the gatha form. The text was compiled by an individual named Hala.
It is an ancient collection of Indian poems in the Maharashtri Prakrit language. The poems are about love. They are written as frank monologues usually by a married woman, or an unmarried girl.
There is a reference to the river Tapi, which cuts through Surat, almost in the centre of India.
subject: History

questionText: 19. With reference to ancient Indian History, ‘Gandakasela and Ganjam’ were:
options: b) silver coins issued by the Chera kings
d) merchant guilds during the Sangam age
explanation:
The Satavahana Empire was a dynasty that ruled parts of present-day India from around 230 BCE to 220 CE. The empire was based in the Deccan region of India, and its capital was located at Pratishthana (present-day Paithan) in Maharashtra.
Vashishtaputra Pulamayi extended the Satavahana power up to the mouth of the Krishna River. He issued coins on which the image of ships was inscribed. They reveal the naval power and maritime trade of the Satavahanas.
Their greatest port was Kalyani on the west Deccan. Gandakasela and Ganjam on the east coast were the other important seaports.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
20. With reference to the Satavahanas, consider the following statements:
1. Satavahanas traded extensively with the Roman empire
2. Nigamas served as prominent towns functioning as centers of trade.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
In the Deccan, the Satavahanas established their independent rule around the 1st century BCE after the decline of the Mauryas. Their rule lasted for about 450 years and they were also known as the Andhras. It was under the Satavahanas that the first state emerged in Deccan.
The founder of the Satavahana dynasty was Simuka. He was succeeded by Krishna, who extended the kingdom up to Nasik in the west. The third king was Sri Satakarni. He conquered western Malwa and Berar. He also performed asvamedha sacrifices.
The greatest ruler of the Satavahana dynasty was Gautamiputra Satakarni. He ruled for a period of 24 years from 106 to 130 A.D. He captured the whole of Deccan and expanded his empire. His achievements were recorded in the Nasik inscription by his mother Gautami Balasri.
Many towns of the Satavahana Empire were centres of trade. The towns were called 'Nigamas'. Paithan, Karle, Kanheri, Junnar and Nasik of Maharashtra, Dhanyaketa of Andhra Pradesh and Vyjayanthi of Uttara Kannada district were the main centres of trade. So, statement 2 is correct.
In the growth of Satavahana Kingdom, it was not just the land, but the sea trade with the Romans played a major role. Ptolemy mentions many ports in the Deccan. The greatest port of the Satavahanas was Kalyani on the west Deccan. The kingdoms of both Satavahanas as well as the Kushans profited from trade with the Roman empire. External trade was carried between South India and the Greek kingdoms. After the ascendancy of the Roman Empire, the Roman trade assumed importance. So, statement 1 is correct.
Satavahana rulers were identified through metronymics (names derived from that of the mother). Although this may suggest that mothers were important, succession to the throne was generally patrilineal.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: 21. With reference to Sangam Age, the terms Mullai, Marutham and Neithal refer to:
options: a) custom duties levied on imports of gemstones
c) villages granted for maintenance of temples
explanation:
Five ecological regions mentioned in ancient Tamil Sangam literature are Kurinji, Mullai, Marutham, Neithal and Paalai. The details of each ecological region are as follow:
Sl. No. Ecological Region Relief Feature (Specific Area)
1. Kurinji Thinai Mountains and slopes (Nilgiris, Kodaikanal, Palani, Kolli and Jawadhu Hills).
2. Mullai Thinai Forests and grasslands (foothills of the Eastern and Western Ghats in Coimbatore, Salem, Tirunelveli, Krishnagiri, Theni and Kanyakumari districts).
3. Marutham Thinai Plains, valleys and agricultural lands (Area from Thanjavur, Tiruvarur, Coimbatore, Tiruchi, Salem, Tirunelveli and Krishnagiri).
4. Neithal Thinai Coastal or seashore (Nagapattinam, Thoothukudi and Kanyakumari districts).
5. Paalai Thinai Parched wasteland or desert (Ramanathapuram, Thoothukudi, Sivaganga and Virudhunagar districts).
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
22. Consider the following statements with reference to Samudragupta:
1. He followed the policies of Grahana and Moksha with respect to South Indian Kings.
2. The Kashmir region remained under his direct control at the end of his reign.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Samudragupta was the son of the Gupta emperor Chandragupta I and the second emperor of the Gupta Empire of ancient India. He is regarded as among the greatest rulers of the dynasty.
Regarding the kings of south India or Dakshinapatha, Samudragupta first captured them (grahana) and then released them (moksha). However, he followed a completely different policy with regard to the kings of Aryavarta. He subjugated them and annexed their territories. So, statement 1 is correct.
At the end of his reign, most parts of north India were directly under the control of Samudragupta, except Kashmir, western Punjab, Rajasthan, Sindh and Gujarat. So, statement 2 is not correct.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
23. Consider the following statements with reference to Allahabad Pillar Inscription:
1. It was composed by Harishena during the Gupta period.
2. It is incised in Sanskrit language and Brahmi script.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Allahabad Pillar Inscription (Prayagraj Prashasti) in prose and verse, is a retrospective glowing account of Samudragupta's personality, personal qualities, greatness and glorious exploits.
It is the most important source which gives us information about the accession and conquests of Samudragupta.
It was composed by his court poet Harishena who composed 33 lines which were engraved on a pillar. It is incised in Sanskrit language and Brahmi script. So, both statements 1 and 2 are correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
24. Consider the following statements with reference to the Gupta society:
1. Fahien’s account mentions the social position of ‘Chandalas’.
2. The position of Shudras improved during this period as they were allowed to listen to the religious recitations.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
The idea of the varna divided social order became the norm. The position of women of higher varna was lower in position than the men of the same varna. The woman in a family was expected to function as an ideal wife and mother. In texts, women were even considered to be of the same category as the Shudras.
Fa-Hien was a Chinese pilgrim who visited India during the reign of Chandragupta II on a religious mission. He traveled by foot from China to India and returned by sea route. It is mentioned in his account that, “Throughout the whole country the people do not kill any living creature, nor drink intoxicating liquor, nor eat onions or garlic. The only exception is that of the Chandalas. That is the name for those who are (held to be) wicked men, and live apart from others. When they enter the gate of a city or a market-place, they strike a piece of wood to make themselves known, so that men know and avoid them, and do not come into contact with them.” So, statement 1 is correct.
However, the position of Shudras improved slightly during this period. They were now permitted to listen to recitations of the Ramayana, Mahabharata, and Puranas but not the Veda, which was still considered sacred. So, statement 2 is correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
25. With reference to the Gupta Empire, consider the following statements:
1. Prakrit was made the official language of the court by the Gupta rulers.
2. Navaratnas were present in the court of Chandragupta Vikramaditya.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Cultural developments during the Gupta period:
Classical Sanskrit' developed during the Guptas. Sanskrit was widely patronised by Gupta rulers, making it the official language of their court. All their inscriptions are written in Sanskrit. So, statement 1 is not correct.
Chandragupta Vikramaditya (Chandragupta-II) had a deep interest in art and culture and had nine gems/Navaratnas present in his court. They were Amarsimha, Dhanvantri, Harisena, Kalidasa, Kahapanaka, Sanku, Varahamihira, Vararuchi and Vetalbhatta. So, statement 2 is correct.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
26. Which of the following information regarding early medieval India can be found in the accounts of the Chinese traveller Hiuen Tsang?
1. The Bodhi tree at Gaya was destroyed by Pushyamitra Sunga.
2. Harshavardhana was a follower of Mahayana Buddhism.
3. Salaries to officials were paid by land grants by Harshavardhana.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation:
Accounts of the Hiuen Tsang: The Chinese traveller Hiuen Tsang travelled in India from 629-645 CE and spent his time at the court of Harshavardhana.
He mentions that some years before 637-638 CE, Sasanka, the Gauda ruler of Bengal had cut down the Bodhi tree at Gaya. It was a sacred symbol of the Buddhists. So, point 1 is not correct.
He speaks about the prosperity of Kannauj under Harshavardhana. He said that the king travelled frequently throughout his kingdom to understand the condition of his subjects. He presents Harshavardhana as a follower of Mahayana Buddhism and he has given a grand description of the Buddhist assembly at Kannauj during his reign. So, statement 2 is correct.
He also mentions that Harshavardhana used to pay salaries to his ministers and officials by land grants. However, the actual evidence of such secular grants has not been yet found. So, statement 3 is correct.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: 27. With reference to Indian History, ‘Madhuban Plate inscription’ is known for:
options: a) earliest epigraphic evidence of practice of sati
b) providing information about Chola ruler Karikala
c) first inscription of emperor Ashoka that mentioned his name
d) tracing the genealogy of Harshavardhana
explanation:
Madhuban Plate inscription is obtained from a place called Madhuban in Ghosi Teshil of Mau district of Uttar Pradesh. This inscription is related to the Harshavardhana.
The language of this inscription is Sanskrit and Script is Brahmi. The date of Harshasamvat (631 CE) is mentioned in this Tampatra Inscription. Madhuban plate inscription dated 631 CE traces the genealogy of Harsha up to four generations.
The Maski Inscription was the first edict of Emperor Ashoka that contained the name Ashoka in it instead of ‘Devanampriya’ or ‘Piyadassi’.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
28. Consider the following pairs with reference to the administration in the Harshavardhan era:
Sl. No. Official Function
1. Kayastha : Spiritual Advisor
2. Prathama kulika : Chief Craftsman
3. Singhanada : Chief Banker
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
explanation:
Harshavardhan ascended the throne in 606 CE. He belonged to the Pushyabhuti dynasty based around Sthanvisvara (modern Thanesar in the Ambala district of Punjab). He oversaw all administrative, legislative, and judicial functions.
He governed his empire on the same lines, as did the Guptas. But during his period the administration became more decentralized and the number of feudatories grew further. In Harsha’s time the officers and the religious persons were paid mainly in land. It encouraged the system of feudalism which grew much more in the post- Harsha period.
Important officers of Harsha’s administration:
Kayasthas: Head of the scribes. So, pair 1 is not correctly matched.
Sarthavaha: Leader of Merchant Caravans
Prathama kulika: Chief Craftsman. So, pair 2 is correctly matched.
Avanti: Officer in charge of both war and peace.
Singhanada: Army's Commander-in-Chief. So, pair 3 is not correctly matched.
Kuntala: Cavalry's commander
Sreshti: Chief Banker or Merchant.
So, only one of the above pairs is correctly matched.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
29. Consider the following:
1. Nagananda
2. Ratnavali
3. Kumarasambhava
4. Priyadarshika
How many of the above are the literary works of Harshavardhana?
explanation:
Harshavardhana was one of the most important Indian emperors of the 7th Century. He ruled from 606 to 647 CE. He became the most successful emperor of the Pushyabhuti dynasty.
He was a great lover of education and learning. He authored 'Ratnavali', 'Priyadarshika' and 'Nagananda'. So, points 1, 2 and 4 are correct.
Ratnavali is a Sanskrit drama about Ratnavali, a beautiful princess, and Udayana, a king. It's a four-act Natika. This text contains one of the earliest textual references to the celebration of Holi, the festival of colours.
Nagananda is among the most acclaimed Sanskrit dramas. The unique characteristic of this drama is the invocation to Buddha in the Nandi verse, which is considered one of the best examples of dramatic compositions.
Priyadarshika is a Sanskrit play and a constructive prelude to a drama.
Kumarasambhava is an epic poem by Kalidasa. It is widely regarded as the finest work of Kalidasa as well as the greatest kavya poem in classical Sanskrit. So, point 3 is not correct.
So, only three of the above are the literary works of Harshavardhana.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: 30. With reference to the post-Gupta period, ‘Jayaskandhavaras’ refers to:
explanation:
In the post-Gupta period, some regions became more powerful than others. For example, the places located in strategically sound areas, like those situated on elevated regions or those surrounded by hills or rivers; regions which were well connected by land and water routes for facilitating the movement of the army or the easy transportation of supplies and provisions for the army, assumed more importance than the older rajdhanis (capitals). These new centres were called Jayaskandhavaras (literally meaning ‘camp of victory’) in epigraphs.
It was from these Jayaskandhavaras that the ruling dynasties issued land grants to religious recipients like the Brahmanas or the monasteries and temples.
Banskhera and the Madhuban copper plates of Harsha were issued from the Jayaskandhavaras of Vardhamanakoti and Kapitthika respectively. Both were probably located somewhere in Uttar Pradesh.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
31. Consider the following pairs:
Sl. No. Literature Author
1. Harshacharita : Banabhatta
2. Amarakosa : Asvaghosha
3. Saundarananda : Harshavardhana
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
explanation:
The early history of Harsha’s reign is reconstructed from a society of Banabhatta who was his court poet and who wrote a book called Harshacharita. This can be supplemented by the account of the Chinese pilgrim Hsuan Tsang. So, pair 1 is correctly matched.
The Gupta empire also saw a memorable compilation of the Amarakosa by Amarasimha who was a luminary in the court of Chandragupta II. So, pair 2 is not correctly matched.
Asvaghosha, a court poet during the reign of king Kanishka was well known for his literature. He wrote Buddhacharita which is a biography of the Buddha. He also composed Saundarananda, which is a fine example of Sanskrit kavya. So, pair 3 is not correctly matched.
So, only one of the above pairs is correctly matched.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
32. With reference to the Samantas during ancient India, consider the following statements:
1. They were the conquered feudatories in the different regions of the Indian subcontinent.
2. They gradually became independent entities and established their own rule.
3. The Maukharis were the samantas of the Gupta kings.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation:
Samanta was the term used for the conquered feudatories during the sixth century. In North India, the earliest use of the term can be seen in the Bengal Inscription and the Barabar Cave Inscription of the Maukhari chief Anantavarman. So, statement 1 is correct.
By the seventh century, there were big landlords or warrior chiefs in different regions of the subcontinent. Existing kings often acknowledged them as their subordinates. They were expected to bring gifts for their kings or overlords, be present at their courts and provide them with military support.
As samantas gained power and wealth, they declared themselves to be maha-Samanta, mahamandaleshvara (the great lord of a “circle” or region) and so on. So, statement 2 is correct.
Sometimes they asserted their independence from their overlords. One such instance was that of the Rashtrakutas in the Deccan. Initially, they were subordinate to the Chalukyas of Karnataka. In the mid-eighth century, Dantidurga, a Rashtrakuta chief, overthrew his Chalukya overlord and performed a ritual called hiranya-garbha (literally, the golden womb).
The Maukharis ruled over Kannauj, a city in western Uttar Pradesh. They were the subordinate rulers of the Guptas. They used the title of samanta. So, statement 3 is correct.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: 33. In the context of Gupta empire, the term ‘Sandivigraha’ refers to:
options: a) officer responsible for revenue collection
b) special courts for adjudication of daily disputes
c) minister in charge of foreign affairs
d) royal decree issued by the kings
explanation:
The Gupta Empire, which existed in ancient India from around 320 to 550 CE, was known for its efficient and effective administration. In the Gupta Empire, the king was directed in his administration by a community and group consisting of a chief minister and a Senapati. There were various names of the empire Rajya, Rashtra, Desha, Mandala, Prithvi and Avani. A high official called ‘Sandivigraha’ is mentioned in the Gupta inscriptions, for ‘foreign affairs.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
34. Consider the following rulers:
1. Narasimhavarman I of Pallava Dynasty
2. Harshavardhana of Vardhan Dyansty
3. Pulakesin II of Chalukyan Dyansty
4. Samudragupta of Gupta Dyansty
The Chinese Buddhist monk, Hiuen Tsang visited the kingdom of how many of the above rulers?
explanation:
Hiuen Tsang or Xuan Zang was a Chinese Buddhist monk, scholar, traveller, and translator who travelled to India in the seventh century and described the interaction between Chinese Buddhism and Indian Buddhism.
He also visited Nalanda university where he spent at least two years. He visited Kannauj which was the capital of Harshavardhana (Vardhan Dyanasty). Harsha invited him to attend a great Buddhist assembly and also Kumbh mela at Prayag. So, point 2 is correct.
Narasimhavarman I (Pallava Dynasty) was also known as Mamalla, which means 'great wrestler'. He wanted to avenge the defeat of his father at the hands of Chalukyan ruler Pulakesin II. His victory over Pulakesin II in the Battle of Manimangalam near Kanchi is mentioned in Kuram copper plates. During his reign, Hiuen Tsang visited the Pallava capital Kanchipuram. His description of Kanchi is vivid. He calls it a big and beautiful city, six miles in circumference. So, point 1 is correct.
The most important ruler of the Chalukyan dynasty was Pulakesin II(Chalukyan Dyansty). The Aihole inscription issued by him gives the details of his reign. The most important event in the reign of Pulakesin II was the visit of Hiuen Tsang to his kingdom. So, point 3 is correct.
The Chinese Buddhist monk, Xuan Zang did not visit the Kingdom of Samudragupta(Gupta Dyansty). So, point 4 is not correct.
So, the Chinese Buddhist monk, Hiuen Tsang visited the kingdom of only three of the above rulers.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
35. Consider the following statements with reference to Harshavardhana:
1. Harsha was the joint ruler of the Pushyabhuti and Maukhari kingdoms.
2. Chalukyan king Pulakesin II defeated Harsha on the banks of Godavari River
3. He successfully resisted the invasion by the Hunas.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
Harsha ascended the throne in 606 CE and belonged to the Pushyabhuti dynasty, also known as Vardhana dynasty, based around Sthanvisvara (modern Thanesar in the Ambala district of Punjab).
Since the Maukharis had no successor, the throne of Kanyakubja was offered to him by the ministers of the Maukharis. He accepted it and, thus, he became the joint ruler of the Pushyabhuti and Maukhari kingdoms. He made Kanyakubja his capital. So, statement 1 is correct.
Pulakesin II defeated Harsha on the banks of Narmada in the winter of 618-619 CE. Pulakesin acquired the title of Parameshvara by defeating sakalottarapathesvara (Harsha). So, statement 2 is incorrect.
Xuan Zang (Hiuen Tsang), the Chinese pilgrim travelled in India during the reign of Harshavardhana. He speaks about the prosperity of Kanauj under Harsha. He says that the king travelled frequently throughout his kingdom to understand the condition of his subjects.
It was during the reign of Kumaragupta, Hunas had invaded India for the first time. The Hunas had attacked the north-western frontier of the Pushyabhuti dynasty when Prabhakaravardhana was ruling; which was successfully resisted by Harshavardhana. So, statement 3 is correct.
Hiuen Tsang says that at the Prayaga Assembly, Harsha worshipped the Buddha, Shiva and the Sun and distributed charity to the followers of all faiths. Besides, the Banskhera and Madhuban copper plates of Harsha record his grant of land to Rigvedin and Samavedin brahmanas. Hence, it can be attributed that Harsha's administration was secular in nature, as he supported all faiths and religions.
So, only two of the above statements are correct.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
36. With reference to the administration during Gupta empire, consider the following statements:
1. It was more decentralised than the Mauryan administration.
2. Uparikara and Udranga were the agricultural taxes levied during the Gupta period.
3. There was an absence of clear and elaborate laws of inheritance.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
King was the central authority in the Mauryan Empire. The Gupta administration was more decentralised as compared to that of the Mauryas. Local authorities, social groups and powerful trade guilds retained significant autonomy in the empire. So, statement 1 is correct.
During the Gupta period, land taxes increased considerably. The land tax called bali varied from 1/4th to 1/6th of the total produce. Two new agricultural taxes that appear in Gupta inscriptions are uparikara and udranga. However, their exact nature is not clear. So, statement 2 is correct.
The judicial system was far more developed under the Gupta rulers than in earlier times. For the first time, civil and criminal laws were clearly demarcated. Disputes connected with various types of property were considered in civil law. Elaborate laws were laid down about inheritance. There were different courts like Karana, Adhikarana, dharmasana, etc. Criminal and civil cases were clearly differentiated from each other. Laws regarding property and inheritance were elaborate. Of course, justice was based on the varna classification in society. For the same kind of crime, culprits belonging to a higher varna (caste) got less punishment than those belonging to a lower varna. Dharmashastras also insisted that local usages and practices of different guilds and castes should be given due weight while dispensing justiceTheft and adultery fell under criminal law. So, statement 3 is not correct.
So, only two of the above statements are correct.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
37. Consider the following:
1. Ritusamhara
2. Meghadutam
3. Malavikagnimitram
4. Kiratarjuniya
How many of the above literary works can be attributed to Kalidasa?
explanation:
Kalidasa was a Sanskrit poet and dramatist in the 5th century CE. He was among the Navaratnas (nine gems) of the court of Gupta king Chandragupta Vikramaditya. Works of Kalidasa:
Raghuvansham: Story of Raghu dynasty including Raghu, Dasharatha and Rama.
Malavikagnimitram: Play involving the love story of King Agnimitra from Shunga Dynasty and his love interest Malavika. So, point 3 is correct.
Vikramorvashiyam: Love story of King Pururavas and an Apsara named Urvashi.
Other works include: Ritusamhara, Meghadutam, Kumarasambhavam, Abhijnanashakuntalam. So, points 1 and 2 are correct.
Kiratarjuniya is an epic poem by Bharavi. It describes the combat between Arjuna and Lord Shiva. So, point 4 is not correct.
So, only three of the above literary works can be attributed to Kalidasa.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
38. Consider the following statements with reference to the Skandagupta:
1. He was the last Gupta ruler to mint silver coins in western India.
2. The Bhitari inscription contains information about his reign.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Skandagupta (454-467 CE): He is generally considered the last important ruler of the Gupta Empire, who ruled over a vast territory.
From the beginning of his reign, he started facing various troubles in his realm. The Bhitari inscription informs that at the very beginning of his reign, Pushyamitras posed trouble. So, statement 2 is correct.
Though he was successful in averting the onslaught of Hunas, these campaigns cost the kingdom dearly. Scholars maintain that his gold coins were not only limited but also had less gold in them as compared with the earlier rulers. He was also the last Gupta ruler to mint silver coins in western India. So, statement 1 is correct.
After Skandgupta's reign, the Gupta power weakened. Soon many subordinate rulers became independent. The later Gupta kings ruled between 475-530 CE. They ruled over small territories and functioned as petty rulers. The Gupta rule finally came to an end by the middle of the sixth century CE.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
39. With reference to the rulers of Pushyabhuti Dynasty, consider the following statements:
1. They were the feudatories of the Mauryas.
2. Rajyavardhana was the founder of Pushyabhuti dynasty.
3. Rise of the Pushyabhuti dynasty was one of the reasons for the fall of the Gupta empire.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
Pushyabhuti Dynasty: They were the feudatories of the Guptas. They called themselves Vardhanas. After the Huna invasions, they assumed independence. So, statement 1 is incorrect.
They first ruled from Thanesar in Haryana and later from Kannauj in Uttar Pradesh.
Harshacharita, accounts of Hiuen-Tsang and some inscriptions and coins provide important information of their rule.
Prabhakarvardhana is said to be the founder of the dynasty. After Prabhakaravardhana's death, his elder son Rajyavardhana came to the throne. Rajyavardhana was succeeded by his brother Harshavardhana. So, statement 2 is incorrect.
Rajavardhana (605-606 CE), the eldest son of Prabhakaravardhana, ascended the throne after his father's death. He was treacherously murdered by Sasanka, the Gauda ruler of Bengal. This resulted in his younger brother Harsha-vardhan becoming the king of Thanesar.
The Gupta empire was undermined by the rise of the feudatories. Pushyabhutis were the feudatories of the Gupta rulers. They tended to declare themselves independent. Similarly, the feudatories in west and east India also declared themselves independent. Hence, Statement 3 is correct
So, only one of the above statements is correct.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
40. Consider the following statements with reference to the Gupta period:
1. Buddhism witnessed an increase in patronage as compared to the Mauryan times.
2. The literature of Devichandraguptam provides a narrative of Gupta political history.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Buddhism no longer received royal patronage in the Gupta period. Buddhism was not so important in the Gupta period as it was in the days of Ashoka and Kanishka. So, statement 1 is not correct.
Mudraraksasa and the Devichandraguptam were written by Vishakhadatta, a 7th-century dramatist. Mudrarakshasa is a political drama that narrates the ascent of king Chandragupta Maurya to power in India. It revolves around the manoeuvres of Chanakya to win over Rakshasa, a minister of the Nandas, to Chandragupta's side.
Devichandraguptam centres on an incident set in the reign of the Gupta king Ramagupta. Fragments of Devichandraguptam were found preserved in a manuscript of Bhoja's Shringara Prakasha, and are relevant to Gupta political history. So, statement 2 is correct.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: 41. Samudragupta followed various strategies with respect to the kings of South India (Dakshinapath) and kings of Aryavarta. With reference to this, which one of the following statements is correct?
options: a) Towards the kings of South India, Samudragupta captured and subsequently released them, while in Aryavarta, he subjugated and annexed Kings’ territories.
b) He first captured (grahana) and then released (moksha) both the kings of Aryavarta and Dakshinapath.
c) He subjugated and annexed the territories of both the kings of Aryavarta and Dakshinapath.
d) He did not try to annex the kingdoms of the Aryavarta, while he captured the kings of the Dakshinapath.
explanation:
Regarding the kings of south India or Dakshinapatha, Samudragupta first captured them (grahana) and then released them (moksha). However, he followed a completely different policy with regard to the kings of Aryavarta. He subjugated them and annexed their territories. Hence, option (a) is the correct answer.
The Prashasti further states that Samudragupta reduced the states in the forest regions to servitude. The frontier kings defeated by him included: Samatata (in southeast Bengal), Kamarupa (Assam), Nepala (Nepal) and others; republican states of the Malavas, Yaudheyas, Madrakas, Abhiras etc. All these rulers paid rich tributes to him and accepted his suzerainty.
The foreign rulers of north-western India like the later Kushanas and the Saka chief and residents of different island countries including Sinhala or Sri Lanka not only surrendered to him but also offered their daughters in marriage.
subject: History

questionText:
42. With reference to the administration of the Guptas, consider the following statements:
1. The territorial units under vishaya were known as the bhukti.
2. The bhuktis were governed by Uparikas directly appointed by the king.
3. Pustapalas were officials who managed and kept records.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
For facilitating the efficient functioning of administration, the kingdom was generally divided into a number of regions or provinces, headed by governors appointed by the king. These provinces were known as the deshas, rashtras or bhuktis. Some of the provinces mentioned in Gupta inscriptions are Tirabhukti (corresponded to north Bihar), Nagarabhukti, Magadhabhukti and Pundravardhanabhukti (corresponded to north Bengal). The bhuktis were governed by Uparikas directly appointed by the king. The territorial units under the provinces (bhukti/desha) were known as vishaya (governed by vishayapati or in some cases by Ayuktaka). Hence, Statement 1 is incorrect and Statement 2 is correct.
Ayuktaka/Vishyapati was appointed by provincial governor. The inscriptions from Bengal indicate that the vishayapati associated himself with Nagarsresthi (head of city merchants), Sarthavaha (caravan leader), PrathamaKulika (head of the artisan community) and Prathamakayastha (head of the kayastha community). Apart from these Pustapalas were officials who managed and kept records. Hence, Statement 3 is correct.
The administrative units below the district were referred differently in different regions, such as — vithi, ashtakuladhikarana, bhumi, pathaka and petha. It seems that these units were placed above several villages and were administrated by different officials such as ayuktakas and vithi-mahattaras. The lowest unit of administration was known as the grama which was headed by a gramika or gramadhyaksha. It is not very clear whether these officials were appointed by higher authorities or selected with the permission of villagers.
Therefore, Option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
43. The Gupta rule came to end by the middle of the sixth century CE. Which of the following are the reasons for the decline of the Gupta Empire?
1. Invasions by Hunas from Central Asia
2. Restoring the authority of local chiefs who had accepted Gupta suzerainty
3. Land grants to brahmana donees and surrendering the revenue and administrative rights in favour of the donees
4. Samanta system in which the samantas ruled as subordinates to the central authority
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
The successors of Chandragupta II had to face the Huna onslaught. Hunas were from Central Asia. They were a branch of the White Huns, the Hephthalites from Central Asia, known in the Indian sources as the Hunas. Initially the Gupta rulers were successful in fighting them back. However, the successors of Kumaragupta could not defend their kingdom. Each wave of Huna invasions made the Gupta rule weaker. Thus, the Huna attacks caused a major blow to Gupta authority particularly in northern and western regions of the empire, Hence, statement 1 is correct.
Skandagupta was facing domestic problems also. This involved court rivalries and the breaking away of the feudatories whose political integration into the metropolitan area of the Gupta state was tenuous. This was because the Guptas had always adopted the policy in the conquered areas to restore the authority of local chiefs or kings once they had accepted Gupta suzerainty. Hence, Statement 2 is correct.
Guptas issued landgrants to brahmana donees and in this process surrendered the revenue and administrative rights in favour of the donees. This practice was bound to reduce revenues. Hence, Statement 3 is correct.
It is believed that the samanta system in which the samantas (minor rulers), who ruled as subordinates to the central authority, started to consolidate itself in the Gupta period. This is also believed to be the reason why Gupta administrative structure became so loose. Hence, Statement 4 is correct.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
44. Consider the following statements:
1. The worship of Yakshas and Yakshis started as a consequence of Buddhism.
2. The reference to Yakshis can be found in the Tamil epic, Silappadhikaram.
3. There is no reference to Yakshas in the Vedic literature.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Yaksha and Yakshi or Yakshinis were forest spirits known from the time of Rigveda. They are seen as the cult of common folk. They could be good and evil. They were generally invoked for attaining wealth, prosperity and beauty. Yaksha worship was very popular before and after the advent of Buddhism and it was assimilated in Buddhism and Jainism. So, statement 1 is not correct.
The main Indian religions of later days appropriated these goddesses to attract the rural people and make them accept these religions without reservation.
The importance given to yakshis in Jainism can be seen from the fact that there is a yakshi for each of the 24 tirthankaras. They are the tirthankaras' guardian deities.
Etymologically, the words yaksha and yakshi have their origins in yas, yak or yaj, meaning mysterious, marvellous, spiritual apparition or semi-divine beings. The worship of Isakki Amman (isakki originating from the word yakshi) is popular to this day in the southern districts of Tamil Nadu. The earliest reference to yakshi in Tamil is found in the Jaina epic Silappadhikaram, which talks of Poongkann Iyakki, that is, a yakshi with eyes soft as flowers. So, statement 2 is correct.
Yaksas have been mentioned many times in passages of Rig Veda and Atharva Veda as the tutelary gods symbolizing the mysterious principle of life and creation. There are many references to Yakshas in Puranas, epic books like Mahabharata, and the works of Kalidasa. So, statement 3 is incorrect.
Yakshi sculptures have been found in Mathura in Uttar Pradesh; Didarganj and Basarh in Bihar; Bhopal, Sanchi and Khajuraho in Madhya Pradesh; Ellora in Maharashtra; Udayagiri and Khandagiri in Odisha; Tiruppanmalai, Vallimalai, Anaimalai, Tirumalai, Samanamalai, Sithamur and Sitharal in Tamil Nadu; Aihole and Shravanabelagola in Karnataka; and Nagarjunakonda, Kondapur, Peddapur and Amaravathi in Andhra Pradesh.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
45. Consider the following:
1. Amarasimha
2. Dhanvantari
3. Varahamihira
4. Vararuchi
How many of the above were Navratnas in the court of Chandragupta II?
explanation:
Chandragupta-II was known for his deep interest in art and culture and nine gems or Navratna adorned his court. The various fields of these 9 gems prove that Chandragupta gave patronage to arts and literature. Brief description about the nine Ratnas is as follows:
Amarasimha: He was a Sanskrit lexicographer and a poet and his Amarkosha is a vocabulary of Sanskrit roots, homonyms and synonyms.
Dhanvantri was a great Physician.
Harisena is known to have composed the Prayag Prasasti or Allahabad Pillar Inscription. The title of this inscription of Kavya, but it has both prose and verse.
Kalidasa is the immortal poet and playwright of India and a peerless genius whose works became famous worldwide in modern world. Major works of Kalidasa are Abhijnana Sakuntalam, Malavikagnimitram, Vikramorvasiyam, Kumarasambhavam, Raghuvamsam, Meghasandesam (Meghadutam).
Kshapanka was an astrologer, Sanku was in the field of Architecture.
Varahamihira (died 587) lived in Ujjain and he wrote three important books: Panchasiddhantika, Brihat Samhita, and Brihat Jataka. The Panchasiddhantaka is a summary of five early astronomical systems including the Surya Siddhanta.
Vararuchi is the name of another gem of Chandragupta Vikramaditya who was a grammarian and Sanskrit scholar. Some historians have identified him with Katyayana. Vararuchi is said to be the author of Prakrit Prakasha, which is first Grammar of the Prakrit Language.
Vetalbhatta was a magician.
Therefore, Option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: 46. This city earlier known as Mahodaya assumed a lot of significance in the politics of early medieval North India. It is located in the fertile plains of the Ganga-Yamuna doab and stood on an elevated area that could be easily fortified. There was a shift of focus from Pataliputra in south Bihar to this city which came to constitute a central theme in the politics of post-Gupta north India. This is the most likely description of:
explanation:
Kanauj, earlier known as Kanyakubja or Mahodaya, situated in the Kannauj district of present Uttar Pradesh assumed a lot of significance in the politics of early medieval North India.
Located in the fertile plains of the Ganga-Yamuna doab, Kanauj stood on an elevated area that could be easily fortified. Thus, despite the fact that it was situated in the plains, due to its favourable location, it could be easily fortified and was secure.
Kanauj was also well-connected by routes going towards east into the Ganges plains as well as with those going to the south. Due to all these factors, it rose to power and became an important nodal point in the north. With this development we see a shift of focus from Pataliputra in south Bihar to Kanauj. The latter also came to constitute a central theme in the politics of post-Gupta north India. Hence, Option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
47. The epithet sakalottara path esvara (the lord of the entire north) was bestowed upon
Which of the following rulers by Pulakesin II of the Chalukyan dynasty?
options: a) Mahendravarman I of Pallava dynasty
b) Harshvardhana of the Pushyabhuti dynasty
explanation:
Pulakeshin II was an Emperor from the Chalukya Dynasty. His capital was at Badami in Karnataka. In the same time period Emperor Harshavardhana ruled entire North India from Kannauj. He wanted to expand into the South by defeating Pulakeshin. However, Pulakeshi was able to successfully defeat Harshavardhan in the battle on the banks of river Narmada.
Harshavardhana, the last ruler of the Pushyabhuti dynasty was also known as Sakalottarapathanatha. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
48. With reference to Satavahana inscriptions, consider the following pairs:
Sl. No. Inscription Mentions
1. Karle inscription Mentions the grant of a village by Gautamiputra Satakarni to the Buddhist monks.
2. Kanheri Caves inscriptions Mentions the reign of Yajna Sri Satakarni
3. Nasik inscription Gives the title of Kshatriyadarpa Mardana (Destroyer of the Pride of Kshatriyas) to Vashishtaputra Pulamayi
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
explanation:
Gautamiputra Satakarni (106-130 AD) was a ruler of the Satavahana Empire in present-day Deccan region of India. He was mentioned as the important and greatest ruler of Satavahana Dynasty.
Described as Ekabrahman in Nasik (by his mother, Gautami Balashri) and Nanaghad inscriptions.
Title of Kshatriyadarpa Mardana (Destroyer of the Pride of Kshatriyas) in Nasik inscription.
Known as the destroyer of the Sakas, Pahlavas and Yavanas.
Patronage to Brahmins: He patronised Brahmanism, but he also donated land to the Buddhist monks. Karle inscription mentions the grant of a village by him to the Buddhist monks. Hence, pair 1 is correctly matched.
The inscriptions at Kanheri Caves mention the reign of Yajna Sri Satakarni (165 – 194 AD). He was the last great ruler of Satavahanas. He conquered Kokan and Malwa from the Shaka rulers. Hence, pair 2 is correctly matched.
Gautamiputra Satakarni (106-130 AD) is described as Ekabrahman in Nasik (by his mother, Gautami Balashri) and Nanaghad inscriptions. He is also given the title of title of Kshatriyadarpa Mardana (Destroyer of the Pride of Kshatriyas) in Nasik inscription. Hence, pair 3 is incorrectly matched.
Therefore, option (b)is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: 49. A ship is represented on this coin issued during the reign of which one of the following Kings?
explanation:
Vashishtaputra Pulamayi (130 – 154 AD)
Sources: Karla Caves’ inscription has mentioned him. His marriage with the daughter of Rudradaman I is mentioned in Junagadh inscriptions.
Contribution: He extended his reign up to the mouth of the Krishna River and issued coins with images of ships inscribed on them.
He repaired the old Amaravathi stupa.
